variants.planted_defect: The Delivery Queue, which every channel adapter depends on to receive delivery jobs, is redesigned from a durable three-broker/two-AZ cluster into a single unreplicated instance in one availability zone, so its failure halts delivery across every channel until someone manually restarts it.
variants.why_it_matters: A single unreplicated queue instance turns one infrastructure fault into a total outage of email, SMS, and push delivery simultaneously, with no automatic recovery path.
variants.detectable_quote: If this instance becomes unavailable, all channel adapters stop receiving delivery jobs until it is manually restarted.

variants.planted_defect: The Preference Service is changed to read template metadata directly from the Template Service's database tables instead of through its API, creating an undeclared, schema-level dependency between two components that are supposed to be independent.
variants.why_it_matters: Bypassing the Template Service's API couples the Preference Service to internal schema details the Template Service can no longer change without breaking a consumer it doesn't know exists.
variants.detectable_quote: the Preference Service reads template metadata directly from the Template Service's database tables rather than calling its API

variants.planted_defect: Preference/opt-out enforcement, which the Notification API already performs, is duplicated a second time inside each Channel Adapter -- a layer that is supposed to contain only provider-specific translation logic, not business rules.
variants.why_it_matters: Putting policy logic in a translation-only layer means the two enforcement points can drift out of sync (e.g. a preference-model change updated in one place but not the other), and every future adapter must re-implement and maintain the same rule.
variants.detectable_quote: Each adapter also re-checks the recipient's channel preferences before sending, independently of the check the Notification API already performed

variants.planted_defect: The Notification API is changed to call the channel provider (e.g. SES, Twilio) synchronously inside the HTTP request handler and block until the provider responds, contradicting the service's own async-by-design goal and using the Delivery Queue only as a retry mechanism.
variants.why_it_matters: Blocking the caller's HTTP request on a third-party provider call ties the API's latency and availability to the provider's, defeating the purpose of decoupling via a queue and risking cascading timeouts under provider slowness.
variants.detectable_quote: calls the resolved channel's provider (e.g., SES, Twilio) synchronously within the same HTTP request

variants.planted_defect: The DeliveryAttempt.status field, previously written exclusively by the owning Channel Adapter, is changed so both the Delivery Queue's retry worker and the Channel Adapter may write it, with no stated rule for which write wins.
variants.why_it_matters: Two independent writers to the same status field with no arbitration rule creates a race condition and destroys any single source of truth for a notification's real delivery state.
variants.detectable_quote: Both the Delivery Queue's retry worker and the relevant Channel Adapter may update the status field to reflect the latest known delivery state.

variants.planted_defect: Templates are redefined to be identified solely by a human-readable template_name with no version or locale field, on the stated assumption that only English copy will ever be needed.
variants.why_it_matters: Without a version or locale key from day one, adding localization or safely rolling out copy changes later requires a breaking schema migration and a coordinated update of every caller, instead of an additive change.
variants.detectable_quote: there is no template version or locale field, since the service only needs to support English-language copy today

variants.planted_defect: The /webhooks/delivery-status endpoint, reachable on the public internet, updates DeliveryAttempt records directly from the callback payload with provider signature verification deferred to a follow-up ticket, meaning it currently trusts unauthenticated external input.
variants.why_it_matters: Without signature verification, anyone who can reach the public endpoint can forge delivery-status callbacks and corrupt DeliveryAttempt records for arbitrary notifications.
variants.detectable_quote: provider signature verification was deferred to a follow-up ticket to keep the initial launch simple

variants.planted_defect: The observability plan is changed so the fully rendered notification body -- including the recipient's email address or phone number -- is written to shared application logs at INFO level, retained for two years, and readable by all engineering staff.
variants.why_it_matters: Logging full PII-bearing message content at a broadly-retained, broadly-accessible log level turns routine debugging access into a large, long-lived exposure surface for recipient contact information.
variants.detectable_quote: the fully rendered notification body — including the recipient's email address or phone number — is included in the log line at INFO level

variants.planted_defect: The staged, feature-flagged, percentage-based rollout with automatic rollback is replaced with an instant, all-clients-at-once cutover with no canary, no gating, and no path back to v1 because v1's infrastructure is torn down immediately.
variants.why_it_matters: An instant flip with no staged exposure and no rollback path means any v2 defect immediately affects 100% of clients with no way to revert, the opposite of a safe migration.
variants.detectable_quote: v1 is disabled and v2 is enabled for all clients simultaneously at 9am on the GA date; there is no canary, no percentage-based ramp, and no feature flag gating the cutover

variants.planted_defect: The shim's CI contract-test suite and sandboxed staging environment are replaced with a design that depends on live downstream provider responses that cannot be reproduced outside production, so validation is reduced to manually replaying real requests against production after each deploy.
variants.why_it_matters: A component that can only be verified by hand against production has no pre-deploy safety net; every shim change ships on faith and regressions are found by customers, not tests.
variants.detectable_quote: it is validated by manually replaying a handful of real v1 requests against production after each deploy

variants.planted_defect: The migration guide's explicit handling of clients who persisted v1 page numbers in saved search URLs, scheduled reports, or bookmarks is removed; only the interactive next-page case is addressed, and usage logs can't distinguish the missed cases from ordinary traffic.
variants.why_it_matters: Saved/scheduled requests using v1 page numbers will silently break under cursor pagination with no detection mechanism and no migration path, since the plan no longer accounts for them.
variants.detectable_quote: it does not address clients who have persisted v1 page numbers in saved search URLs, scheduled report configs, or bookmarked links

variants.planted_defect: The OAuth2/JWT migration -- which requires clients to handle token refresh, clock skew, key rotation, and revocation -- is described as a one-line header swap needing no additional client-side handling, dropping all mention of the shared auth library and refresh/clock-skew logic that actually makes this work.
variants.why_it_matters: Framing a stateful, refresh-and-rotation-dependent auth protocol as a trivial header change will lead client integrators to under-build their integration and hit production failures from expired tokens and clock skew that the plan never warned them about.
variants.detectable_quote: since this is just a header format change, no additional client-side handling is expected

variants.planted_defect: The isolated v1-compat/ adapter module is replaced by inline `if request.version == 'v1'` branches scattered throughout the v2 handlers themselves, so legacy-translation logic is mixed directly into the code every v2 request depends on.
variants.why_it_matters: Scattering legacy-compat branches through the main v2 codebase instead of isolating them means every future v2 change risks touching legacy-only code paths, and removing v1 support later requires hunting down conditionals across the whole handler layer instead of deleting one module.
variants.detectable_quote: each v2 handler includes an `if request.version == 'v1': ...` branch that inlines the legacy request/response translation for that endpoint

variants.planted_defect: Despite a shared cursor-kit library already used by four other internal APIs for cursor encoding/validation/expiry, this migration builds its own bespoke base64 cursor format from scratch because the team finds cursor-kit's interface too complicated.
variants.why_it_matters: Rejecting the platform's shared, battle-tested cursor library for a bespoke one-off means reinventing cursor validation and expiry handling, missing whatever hardening cursor-kit already has, and adding a second cursor format for the rest of the company to reason about.
variants.detectable_quote: this migration will implement its own bespoke base64 cursor encoding and validation specific to this API

variants.planted_defect: v2 drops the per-request ownership check against the resource id (present in v1) to save a database round-trip, assuming the API gateway enforces ownership instead, while simultaneously switching resource ids from opaque UUIDs to sequential integers.
variants.why_it_matters: Sequential, guessable resource ids combined with no per-request ownership check is a textbook IDOR: any authenticated caller can enumerate ids and access or mutate other tenants' resources.
variants.detectable_quote: the per-request ownership check against the resource id in the path (present in v1) is removed in v2; ownership is assumed to already be enforced by the API gateway's routing rules

variants.planted_defect: The per-client rate limit on the /oauth/token endpoint is removed; clients may request tokens as often as they want because adding a limiter was deprioritized for launch.
variants.why_it_matters: An unthrottled token-issuance endpoint is a direct enabler of credential-stuffing and brute-force attacks against client secrets, with no rate-based control to slow or detect an attack.
variants.detectable_quote: The `/oauth/token` endpoint has no request rate limiting; clients may request new tokens as often as their integration needs

variants.planted_defect: The deprovisioning checklist drops the contractor-offboarding requirement entirely, even though contractors have their own onboarding requirement in section 3 and are explicitly named as in-scope in section 2.
variants.why_it_matters: A SOC2 access-control readiness checklist that never addresses how contractor access is revoked leaves a whole population's offboarding untested and unattested for the audit.
variants.detectable_quote: Contractor onboarding: access is requested by the sponsoring manager, approved by IT Security, time-boxed to the contract end date by default

variants.planted_defect: The quarterly review process is rewritten to source its user list solely from the identity provider (Okta), silently dropping the earlier explicit coverage of local-account legacy tools -- even though section 2 states those systems are in scope.
variants.why_it_matters: An access review process that implicitly assumes every system is covered by the IdP export will systematically miss reviewing the local, non-federated legacy accounts that section 2 says are in scope, with no one noticing because nothing flags the gap.
variants.detectable_quote: Quarterly access reviews are performed by exporting the user list from the identity provider (Okta) and having the relevant manager attest to continued need for access.

variants.planted_defect: The privileged-session idle-timeout/re-authentication requirement is dropped from the privileged access checklist, leaving MFA, credential rotation, and break-glass review as the only privileged-access controls.
variants.why_it_matters: Without an idle-timeout control, an unattended authenticated privileged session (production or database admin) stays open indefinitely, a standard SOC2 control gap an auditor would flag.
variants.detectable_quote: Privileged account credentials (service account keys, shared admin passwords) are rotated every 90 days. Owner: Platform Engineering.

variants.planted_defect: The provisioning checklist's explicit rehire case (recreating access from scratch and confirming no orphaned entitlements from a prior tenure) is removed, leaving only new-hire, contractor, and internal-transfer cases enumerated.
variants.why_it_matters: Without an explicit rehire procedure, a returning former employee could be provisioned via the ordinary new-hire flow while old, unrevoked entitlements or group memberships from their prior tenure are never checked for and silently persist.
variants.detectable_quote: Internal transfer / role change: the employee's manager and the new team's manager jointly confirm the target access profile; prior role's access not needed in the new role is removed within 5 business days.

variants.planted_defect: The temporary-access-exception process still requires a documented business need and a time-box, but the approval, tracking, and expiration of exceptions is no longer assigned to any named role or team, unlike every other step in the document which names an owner.
variants.why_it_matters: An exception process with no accountable owner for tracking and expiring grants means temporary elevated access can quietly become permanent with no one responsible for catching it.
variants.detectable_quote: the process for approving, tracking, and expiring these exceptions is not formally assigned to a specific role or team

variants.planted_defect: The internal review dashboard is changed to render each user-supplied, free-text access-request justification as raw HTML (instead of escaped plain text), so any requester-supplied markup is executed/rendered in the reviewer's browser.
variants.why_it_matters: Rendering unsanitized user input as raw HTML in an internal dashboard is a stored injection (XSS) vector: any requester can embed script or malicious markup in their justification field and have it execute in a reviewer's session.
variants.detectable_quote: the internal review dashboard renders each justification as raw HTML so reviewers can see any formatting (bold, links) the requester included

variants.planted_defect: Evidence collection is changed from an internally built, read-only export script to a third-party spreadsheet automation add-in sourced from an unvetted marketplace listing that is granted standing admin access to the identity provider's console.
variants.why_it_matters: Granting an unvetted third-party add-in standing admin access to the identity provider console hands a supply-chain attacker (or a compromised/malicious add-in) the ability to create, modify, or delete any identity in the company.
variants.detectable_quote: a third-party spreadsheet automation add-in, sourced from an unvetted marketplace listing, that is granted standing admin access to the identity provider's console

variants.planted_defect: Exported user-access reports -- which for HR-linked systems include employee SSNs -- are moved from an access-restricted evidence folder into the company's general-purpose shared drive, accessible to all employees, in the name of transparency.
variants.why_it_matters: Storing SSN-bearing access-review exports in a company-wide-accessible shared drive turns a compliance evidence artifact into a broad, unnecessary exposure of sensitive PII to every employee.
variants.detectable_quote: stored in the company's general-purpose shared drive folder, accessible to all employees, in the interest of transparency
